Study Title: Exome sequencing identifies the cause of a mendelian disorder.
Authors: Ng SB, Buckingham KJ, Lee C, Bigham AW, Tabor HK, Dent KM, Huff CD, Shannon PT, Jabs EW, Nickerson DA, Shendure J, Bamshad MJ
Author Affiliations: Department of Genome Sciences, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington, USA.
Key Finding: CDC27 was identified as a candidate gene but later determined to be a false positive due to processed pseudogenes.
Key Finding Citation: The asterisk indicates that a second gene, CDC27 , was also identified as a candidate gene but on manual review it appears to be multiple copies of a processed pseudogene that cause a false positive.

Study Title: Exome sequencing identifies the cause of a mendelian disorder.
Authors: Ng SB, Buckingham KJ, Lee C, Bigham AW, Tabor HK, Dent KM, Huff CD, Shannon PT, Jabs EW, Nickerson DA, Shendure J, Bamshad MJ
Author Affiliations: Department of Genome Sciences, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington, USA.
Key Finding: Mutations in DHODH, which encodes dihydroorotate dehydrogenase in the pyrimidine de novo biosynthesis pathway, cause Miller syndrome.
Key Finding Citation: Filtering against public SNP databases and a small number of HapMap exomes for genes with two novel variants in each of the four cases identified a single candidate gene, DHODH , which encodes a key enzyme in the pyrimidine de novo biosynthesis pathway.
Variant: Mutations including missense variants and a 1-bp indel were identified, with each parent of an affected individual being a heterozygous carrier.
Variant Citation: Collectively, 11 different mutations in 6 kindreds were identified in DHODH by a combination of exome and targeted resequencing ( Table 4 and Figure 2 ).

Study Title: Exome sequencing identifies the cause of a mendelian disorder.
Authors: Ng SB, Buckingham KJ, Lee C, Bigham AW, Tabor HK, Dent KM, Huff CD, Shannon PT, Jabs EW, Nickerson DA, Shendure J, Bamshad MJ
Author Affiliations: Department of Genome Sciences, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington, USA.
Key Finding: The c.G454A variant found in kindred 1 was predicted to be damaging.
Key Finding Citation: However, because the other variant found in kindred 1, c.G454A , was predicted to be damaging, as was every other novel DHODH variant identified, DHODH was the only candidate gene for Miller syndrome under a dominant model in a comparison of kindreds 1, 2, and 3.
Variant Citation: However, because the other variant found in kindred 1, c.G454A , was predicted to be damaging, as was every other novel DHODH variant identified, DHODH was the only candidate gene for Miller syndrome under a dominant model in a comparison of kindreds 1, 2, and 3.

Study Title: Exome sequencing identifies the cause of a mendelian disorder.
Authors: Ng SB, Buckingham KJ, Lee C, Bigham AW, Tabor HK, Dent KM, Huff CD, Shannon PT, Jabs EW, Nickerson DA, Shendure J, Bamshad MJ
Author Affiliations: Department of Genome Sciences, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington, USA.
Key Finding: The c.G605A variant was found in both siblings of kindred 1 and was predicted to be benign by PolyPhen.
Key Finding Citation: Review of predicted biophysical consequences of DHODH variants revealed that the effect of one variant, c.G605A , found in both siblings in kindred 1, was benign.
Variant Citation: Review of predicted biophysical consequences of DHODH variants revealed that the effect of one variant, c.G605A , found in both siblings in kindred 1, was benign.

Key Finding: The c1036T mutation was shared between two unrelated individuals with Miller syndrome of different geographical ancestry.
Key Finding Citation: One mutation, c1036T , was shared between two unrelated individuals with Miller syndrome who are of different self-identified geographical ancestry.
Variant Citation: One mutation, c1036T , was shared between two unrelated individuals with Miller syndrome who are of different self-identified geographical ancestry.

Study Title: Exome sequencing identifies the cause of a mendelian disorder.
Authors: Ng SB, Buckingham KJ, Lee C, Bigham AW, Tabor HK, Dent KM, Huff CD, Shannon PT, Jabs EW, Nickerson DA, Shendure J, Bamshad MJ
Author Affiliations: Department of Genome Sciences, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington, USA.
Key Finding: Mutations in DNAH5 were identified in two siblings as the cause of primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) occurring alongside Miller syndrome.
Key Finding Citation: Accordingly, exome analysis revealed that both siblings in kindred 1 have, in addition to Miller syndrome, PCD due to mutations in DNAH5 .

Study Title: Exome sequencing identifies the cause of a mendelian disorder.
Authors: Ng SB, Buckingham KJ, Lee C, Bigham AW, Tabor HK, Dent KM, Huff CD, Shannon PT, Jabs EW, Nickerson DA, Shendure J, Bamshad MJ
Author Affiliations: Department of Genome Sciences, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington, USA.
Key Finding: Sonic hedgehog (Shh) expression is regulated by NF-kappaB, and its disruption may contribute to limb malformations in Miller syndrome.
Key Finding Citation: Shh controls, in part, anterior-posterior patterning of the digits and Shh-/- knockout mice fail to form digits 2-5 29 .

Study Title: Exome sequencing identifies the cause of a mendelian disorder.
Authors: Ng SB, Buckingham KJ, Lee C, Bigham AW, Tabor HK, Dent KM, Huff CD, Shannon PT, Jabs EW, Nickerson DA, Shendure J, Bamshad MJ
Author Affiliations: Department of Genome Sciences, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington, USA.
Key Finding: The paper notes that TNF-alpha production is repressed by inhibition of NF-kappaB, which may be relevant to the developmental defects observed in Miller syndrome.
Key Finding Citation: Indeed, open eye is a defect observed in mice with targeted disruption of TNF-alpha 28 C.
